title: The Road Racing Training Guide
subtitle: How to prepare for gran fondos and road races — training, fueling, and race execution
meta_description: Road racing training guide: periodization, fueling, race-week protocol, and in-race decision making for gran fondos and road races.

steps.label: Base (Weeks 1-4)
steps.content: Establish aerobic foundation. 70-85% volume in Z1-{{Z2}}. {{CTL}} rising steadily (+3-5/wk). {{TSB}} slightly negative (-5 to -15). Week 4 = recovery week (60% volume). Feels boring but doable—you should finish most rides feeling like you could do more.

steps.label: Build (Weeks 5-10)
steps.content: Layer intensity, develop race-specific fitness. 60-70% Z1-Z2, 30-40% Z3-Z6. Two to three quality sessions per week. The magic happens here: deliberately dig a fatigue hole ({{TSB}} goes to -25), recover just enough, then dig another hole. This is where fitness sharpens.

steps.label: Peak/Taper (Weeks 11-12)
steps.content: Shed fatigue, maintain sharpness. Volume drops 30-50%. Intensity stays high but duration decreases. Week 11: {{TSB}} rises to +5 to +10. Week 12: TSB targets +15 to +25 for race day. Fitness doesn't improve—the goal is to remove the fatigue layer so you can express the fitness you've built.

blocks.content: Notice the shape: volume peaks early, intensity peaks late, both taper together. Base fitness takes weeks of accumulated volume to build but decays slowly. Race sharpness takes hard efforts to develop but fatigues you quickly. The taper strips away the fatigue layer while preserving both. Every serious training plan follows this arc.
Zoom out further and the same periodization principles apply to an entire season—multiple race targets, off-season base, and strategic recovery blocks.
Plan backwards from your A-race. Everything else—B races, training camps, recovery weeks—serves that primary goal.

ayahuasca.content: A simple 8-week build is enough. Four weeks of gradually increasing volume, one recovery week, then three more weeks building toward your event. Don't overcomplicate it.

finisher.content: Plan a 12-week block: 4 weeks base building, 4 weeks adding intensity, 3 weeks race-specific work, 1 week taper. Each phase builds on the last.

competitor.content: Use a 16-week periodized plan with distinct base, build, and peak phases. Include a mid-plan recovery week and a proper 10-day taper. Test your fitness at a B-race mid-plan.

podium.content: Structure your season around A-races with 20-week macro cycles. Include base, build, peak, and taper mesocycles. Schedule B-races as training stimulus. Plan recovery blocks between peaks.

steps.label: Pre-Race (3-4 hrs before)
steps.content: 2-3g carbs/kg bodyweight. Low fat, low fiber. Familiar foods only. 500ml water with meal.

steps.label: Starting Line (30-60 min before)
steps.content: One gel (24g carbs) with 200ml water. Tops off blood glucose.

steps.label: 0-30 Minutes
steps.content: Focus on pacing, positioning, settling in. Sip on drink. Heart rate is spiking, gut blood flow is reduced.

steps.label: 30-60 Minutes
steps.content: First gel or equivalent (24g carbs). Start your fueling clock. Hydrate consistently.

steps.label: Every 45-60 Minutes After
steps.content: Consume carbs consistently by the clock. Target rate depends on race duration: 80-100g/hr for races under 4 hours, 60-80g/hr for 4-8 hour events, 50-70g/hr for 8-12 hours, and 40-60g/hr for ultra events (12+ hours). At lower intensities fat becomes your primary fuel and GI tolerance is the limiter. Set a timer. Your brain will be stupid. It will forget to eat.

blocks.content: Every milestone on this timeline is non-negotiable. Miss the pre-race breakfast window and you start in a glycogen hole. Skip the first-hour fueling because you feel fine and you are building a debt you will pay at mile 60. Follow it mechanically.
Set a timer. Seriously. Your brain will be stupid. It will forget to eat. It will lie to you and say "I'm fine, I'll eat at the next aid station." The timer removes decision-making.
Your gut is trainable just like your muscles. If you never eat during training rides, your gut won't tolerate eating during races.

steps.label: Weeks 1-4 (Base Phase)
steps.content: Practice eating real food on easy rides. Start with 40-50g carbs per hour, mostly solid. Build tolerance gradually. SGLT1 transporters can double in 2 weeks of consistent gut training (GSSI).

steps.label: Weeks 5-8 (Build Phase)
steps.content: Increase toward your race-day target rate. Mix of liquids and solids. Practice eating at tempo pace. Your gut adapts to what you train it for.

steps.label: Weeks 9-10 (Peak Training)
steps.content: Hit your race-day target rate (see duration-scaled targets in your prep kit). Race nutrition only (gels, drink, bars you'll use on race day). Practice at race pace. For ultra events (12+ hours), include savory and mixed-macro foods alongside gels.

steps.label: Race Week
steps.content: Stick with what worked in training. No new products. Trust your gut (literally).

ayahuasca.content: Start by eating a small snack 30 minutes before rides. If that sits well for two weeks, try eating during rides. Your gut adapts just like your legs — gradually.

finisher.content: Spend 4 weeks increasing on-bike fueling by 10g/hour per week. Start at 30g/hour and work up to 60g. If you get stomach issues, back off for a week then try again.

competitor.content: Dedicate your long rides to gut training: increase carb intake by 10-15g per hour each week until you reach 80-90g. Practice with race-day products. Log what works and what doesn't.

podium.content: Systematic gut training protocol: Week 1-2 at 60g/hr, Week 3-4 at 75g/hr, Week 5-6 at 90g/hr, Week 7-8 push to 100-120g/hr. Track GI symptoms on a 1-5 scale. Identify your personal ceiling.

blocks.content: When things go sideways (and at some point they will), follow protocol instead of panic. Pre-planned responses beat real-time decision-making when your brain is running on fumes.

items.title: Flat Tire
items.content:
1. Stay calm—signal, drift to the side, stop somewhere safe
2. Big fondos run neutral service on the main road — know from the race guide whether yours does, and how long the wait usually is
3. Fixing it yourself is almost always faster: tube swap, CO2, go
4. Check the tire casing for the cause before installing the tube — glass or wire still in the casing flats you again in 10 minutes
5. Inflate, spin the wheel, verify no rub
6. Resume at Z2 for 2-3 minutes before chasing
7. Your group is gone. Don't burn 20 minutes of matches getting it back—work with the next group that comes through

items.title: Dropped from Group
items.content:
1. Don't panic—emotional response costs more energy than you have
2. Assess honestly: were you overextended or did they surge?
3. Find YOUR sustainable pace (probably Z2-low Z3)
4. Look for riders at similar pace within 30-60 seconds
5. Form new working group if possible—communicate intentions
6. If solo, accept it—focus on YOUR race, not theirs
7. Use solo time gaps to fuel and recover without group pressure

items.title: Bonking
items.content:
1. STOP pushing hard immediately—don't try to push through
2. Sit down if possible (don't stand around)
3. Consume 2-3 gels or 200-300 calories FAST
4. Wait 15-20 minutes MINIMUM
5. Let sugar hit bloodstream—this takes time, be patient
6. Drink 16oz water with gels
7. Resume at Z1-Z2 pace ONLY
8. Fuel aggressively for next hour to rebuild glycogen stores

items.title: Cramping
items.content:
1. Consume sodium (salt tabs or pretzels/chips) + 16oz water immediately
2. Stretch gently—don't force it or you'll tear muscle fibers
3. Drop to Z1-Z2 for 10-15 minutes minimum
4. Back off intensity by 10-15% even after cramps resolve
5. Continue aggressive sodium intake rest of race
6. Don't resume hard efforts until cramping completely resolved

steps.label: Monday (7 days out)
steps.content: 60-90 minutes Z2 endurance. Keep legs moving, nothing hard. Optional: 3 × 1 min Z4 to keep neuromuscular system awake.

steps.label: Tuesday (6 days out)
steps.content: 45-60 minutes Z2. Maybe 2 × 3 min G Spot if you're feeling restless. Don't go hard.

steps.label: Wednesday (5 days out)
steps.content: Rest day or 30-45 minutes very easy spin. The last hard workout was 4-5 days ago. Everything now is just maintenance.

steps.label: Thursday (4 days out)
steps.content: 45-60 minutes Z2. Include 3-5 × 30 seconds Z5-Z6 (openers to keep legs sharp). Last real ride before race.

steps.label: Friday (3 days out)
steps.content: Travel day (if traveling) or rest day. Optional 30-minute easy spin if legs feel heavy.

steps.label: Saturday (2 days out)
steps.content: 30-45 minutes Z2 with 3 × 1 min Z4 openers. Course preview if racing Sunday. No heroics.

steps.label: Sunday (1 day out)
steps.content: 20-30 minutes very easy spin or complete rest. Do what's worked for you previously.

blocks.content: Resist the urge to squeeze in one more hard session. Your fitness is locked in — it was determined weeks ago. This week is about arriving at the start line rested, organized, and mentally calm. Every decision should reduce stress, not add it. Trust the work you have already done.

ayahuasca.content: Take 3-4 days completely easy before your race. One short ride two days out to keep your legs loose. Otherwise, rest. You can't gain fitness this week, but you can definitely lose it by overdoing it.

finisher.content: 7-day taper: reduce volume by 40% but keep one short, sharp effort (10 minutes at race pace) 3 days out. This keeps your legs responsive without adding fatigue. Rest day before race day.

competitor.content: 10-day taper: drop volume 50%, maintain intensity in two short opener sessions (5 days and 2 days out). Each opener: 3x5 minutes at threshold with full recovery. Keep your routine otherwise normal.

podium.content: 12-14 day structured taper: gradual volume reduction (40% week 1, 60% week 2) with strategic openers. Include one race-simulation effort 5 days out (20 min at race pace on similar terrain). Two full rest days before race day.

items.title: Final Bike Check (Thursday or Friday)
items.content:
**Drivetrain:** Chain clean and lubed. Shifting smooth across all gears — you'll use every one of them on the climbs. No chain slip under power.

**Tires:** Check for cuts and embedded glass. Fresh sealant if tubeless. Pressure set for the descent, not the flat — overinflated tires on a wet alpine descent is how races end early.

**Brakes:** Pads with life left in them. A 20-minute descent eats pads. Levers firm.

**Cockpit:** Computer charged and mounted. Bottles/cages secure — bottle ejection on rough pavement is real.

**Don't:** Install a new chain race week. Swap to race wheels you haven't ridden. Add new untested equipment.

items.title: Race Day Bag (Pack Friday or Saturday)
items.content:
**Essentials:** Two spare tubes, CO2 cartridges (2-3) or mini pump, tire levers, multi-tool with chain breaker, quick link.

**Nutrition:** Gels, bars, chews—whatever you practiced with. Bottles pre-mixed. Know the feed-zone locations and what they stock; plan to carry what the gaps demand.

**Other:** Sunscreen, chamois cream, arm warmers and a vest for the early start, rain jacket if the forecast says so. Mountain fondos start cold and finish hot — pockets earn their keep.

**Race essentials:** Bib number and pins, timing chip, ID and emergency contact info.

blocks.content: Weather changes your gear strategy completely. A 55°F start with 15 mph wind feels very different from a 75°F calm morning—and your clothing choices should reflect that.
Scout the forecast Thursday morning. Lock in your plan by Friday. Do not second-guess Saturday morning—the stress of indecision costs more energy than being slightly overdressed.

ayahuasca.content: Ride whatever bike you have — don't buy new gear race week. Make sure your tires hold air, your brakes stop you on a long descent, and you have a spare tube. That's your equipment checklist.

finisher.content: Test your race-day setup on a training ride at least 2 weeks out. Gearing for the steepest climb, saddle bag contents, nutrition placement. Don't change anything in the final week — race with what you've trained with.

competitor.content: Gear for the course: check the gradient profile and make sure your easiest gear lets you stay seated on the steepest sustained section. Decide on race wheels two weeks out and train on them. Lay out all race gear the night before — kit, nutrition, tools, electronics.

podium.content: Study every climb and descent on video or ride them beforehand. Wheel and gearing choices per course profile. Know the feed zones and plan which to skip. Have a mechanical plan: which groups to wait for if service is slow, and when to abandon the lead group to save the race.

blocks.content: Wake up **3 hours before race start** if possible. This gives time for coffee to do its job and morning bathroom routine to complete. Everything from here is logistics, not training.

steps.label: 3 Hours Before Start
steps.content: Wake up. Coffee if you normally drink it (not the time to change habits). Breakfast: 100-150g carbs, minimal fat, minimal fiber. Oatmeal with banana and honey, bagel with jam, or white rice with a pinch of salt.

steps.label: 90 Minutes Before
steps.content: Start sipping sports drink. Begin hydrating and topping off fuel. Apply chamois cream and sunscreen.

steps.label: 60 Minutes Before
steps.content: Arrive at start area. Check in, use porta-potty. Get your head right.

steps.label: 45 Minutes Before
steps.content: Light warmup ride: 10-15 minutes easy spinning. Wake up the legs.

steps.label: 30 Minutes Before
steps.content: Final gear check: bottles filled and secured, nutrition in pockets, tire pressure verified, drivetrain sounds good. Run through your [performance statements](#mental-training-race-tactics) mentally.

steps.label: 20 Minutes Before
steps.content: Openers near the start: 2-3 x 30 seconds at race effort with easy spinning between. Gets HR up, legs awake, reminds body what fast feels like.

steps.label: 10 Minutes Before
steps.content: Roll to start line. Find your position. Deep breath.

steps.label: 5 Minutes Before
steps.content: 6-2-7 breathing if anxious. You're ready. Trust your training.

blocks.prompt: It's race morning. You slept terribly — maybe 4-5 hours of broken sleep. Pre-race nerves kept you awake. You feel groggy and anxious. What do you do?

options.label: Panic — consider DNS because you're not well-rested enough
options.result: Overreaction. One night of poor sleep doesn't significantly impair performance if you've been sleeping well all week. Research consistently shows this. The sleep you banked Monday-Friday matters more than Saturday night.

options.label: Execute your morning protocol exactly as planned, trust the sleep you banked earlier in the week
options.result: Correct approach. Pre-race insomnia is universal. Your body has plenty of stored recovery from the week. Stick to your routine: eat, hydrate, warm up, execute. Adrenaline will handle the rest once the gun goes off.

options.label: Double your caffeine intake to compensate for the poor sleep
options.result: Bad idea. More caffeine than your normal amount risks GI distress, jitteriness, and an elevated heart rate before you even start. Stick with your normal amount. Race day is not the time to change habits.

ayahuasca.content: Wake up early enough to eat breakfast and use the bathroom without rushing. Have coffee if that's your routine. Arrive at the venue with 45 minutes to spare. That's it.

finisher.content: 3 hours before start: eat your tested pre-race meal (500-800 calories, carb-heavy). 90 minutes before: arrive, set up, warm up with a 15-minute easy spin. 30 minutes before: final bathroom break, sip electrolytes.

competitor.content: Follow your rehearsed race-morning protocol. Timed nutrition at 3 hours and 1 hour pre-start. 20-minute structured warm-up including 3 efforts at race pace. Check-in, staging, and mental preparation in the final 15 minutes.

podium.content: Execute your race-morning checklist exactly as rehearsed. Timed wake-up for optimal carb loading. Progressive warm-up protocol: 10 min easy, 5 min tempo, 3x30s at race pace with full recovery. Final equipment check. Pre-staging tactical positioning.

items.label: Keep Moving (5-10 Minutes)
items.detail: Spin in Z1 after crossing the finish line. Let heart rate drop gradually. Stopping abruptly causes blood to pool in your legs.

items.label: Refuel Immediately (Within 30 Minutes)
items.detail: 20-30g protein, 60-100g carbs, 500-750ml fluid with electrolytes. Chocolate milk, protein shake with banana, or whatever the finish line has.

items.label: Rehydrate Aggressively
items.detail: You lost 1-4 liters of fluid. Drink 500-1000ml within the first hour. Add electrolytes. If pee is dark yellow by evening, keep drinking.

ayahuasca.content: Eat whatever sounds good. Drink water. Sit down. Celebrate that you finished. That's your entire post-race protocol. The recovery science can wait — enjoy this moment.

finisher.content: Within 30 minutes: 50g carbs + 20g protein (chocolate milk works). Gentle walk for 10 minutes to flush your legs. Celebrate, then start hydrating with electrolytes for the next 4-6 hours.

competitor.content: Immediate recovery protocol: 1g/kg carbs + 0.3g/kg protein within 30 minutes. Active cool-down spin for 15 minutes. Compression boots if available. Begin structured recovery nutrition for the next 24 hours.

podium.content: Execute your rehearsed recovery protocol: recovery drink immediately, active cool-down, compression therapy. Log race data and subjective RPE before you forget. Begin carb reloading (8-10g/kg over 24 hours). Schedule a recovery massage within 48 hours.
